title: Do I have to play it?
summary: An old woman has not touched her late husband's piano in fourteen months. She calls a tuner. She has one question.

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: Mrs Hoffmann, good morning. Where is the piano?

body.speaker: Mrs Hoffmann
body.text: In the living room. By the window.

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: Old, but a good one. May I open the top?

body.speaker: Mrs Hoffmann
body.text: Please.

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: When was it last tuned?

body.speaker: Mrs Hoffmann
body.text: My husband tuned it himself. He played it. He died last year.

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: I am sorry.

body.speaker: Mrs Hoffmann
body.text: Thank you. The piano has not been played for fourteen months.

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: I can hear that. The strings are tired.

body.speaker: Mrs Hoffmann
body.text: Can you fix it?

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: Yes. Three hours. Some keys are not right. The B and the C-sharp in the middle. I will work slowly.

body.speaker: Mrs Hoffmann
body.text: May I ask one thing?

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: Please.

body.speaker: Mrs Hoffmann
body.text: After you tune it — do I have to play it?

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: No.

body.speaker: Mrs Hoffmann
body.text: Really?

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: A piano does not need to be played. It needs to be in tune. Some people tune a piano and never touch it again. The piano is ready. The person is not ready. Both are okay.

body.speaker: Mrs Hoffmann
body.text: I do not play.

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: I know.

body.speaker: Mrs Hoffmann
body.text: I called you because my granddaughter is coming. She is twelve. She just started lessons. I want her to find a piano that is in tune.

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: That is a good reason.

body.speaker: Mrs Hoffmann
body.text: When she comes, the piano will be ready. I do not need to play.

body.speaker: Mr Bauer
body.text: You do not. Now please — two coffees. I work better with coffee.

annotations.meaningVi: Tôi có thể... không? (cách hỏi lịch sự)
Tôi xin chia buồn (lời chia buồn khi nghe tin buồn)
đúng âm, được lên dây chuẩn
vừa mới bắt đầu (just + quá khứ đơn: nhấn mạnh gần đây)
sửa, khắc phục (fix it = sửa nó)
mệt (ở đây dùng nghĩa bóng: dây đàn mất lực, không còn căng đúng)

grammarNotes.title: 'Have to' for obligation; 'do not have to' for absence of obligation
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Have to + động từ nguyên thể' diễn tả nghĩa vụ. Câu hỏi: 'Do I have to + V?' Trong bài: 'Do I have to play it?' Câu trả lời 'No.' tương đương 'You do not have to play it.' — không bắt buộc. Lưu ý 'have to' khác với 'must': 'must' là cảm thấy bên trong, 'have to' thường do hoàn cảnh bên ngoài. Phủ định 'do not have to' nghĩa là 'không cần / không bắt buộc'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Have to + base verb' means it is necessary. Question form: 'Do I have to + verb?' The dialogue: 'Do I have to play it?' The reply 'No.' means 'You do not have to play it' — no obligation. 'Have to' usually comes from outside circumstances; 'must' is more from inside the speaker. The negative 'do not have to' specifically means 'not necessary,' not 'forbidden.'

translationVi:
Ông Bauer: Bà Hoffmann, chào bà. Đàn ở đâu ạ?
Bà Hoffmann: Trong phòng khách. Cạnh cửa sổ.
Ông Bauer: Cũ, nhưng đàn tốt. Tôi mở nắp được chứ ạ?
Bà Hoffmann: Mời ông.
Ông Bauer: Lần cuối đàn được chỉnh dây là khi nào?
Bà Hoffmann: Chồng tôi tự chỉnh. Ông ấy chơi đàn. Ông ấy mất năm ngoái.
Ông Bauer: Tôi xin chia buồn.
Bà Hoffmann: Cảm ơn ông. Đàn không được chơi mười bốn tháng rồi.
Ông Bauer: Tôi nghe thấy. Dây mệt rồi.
Bà Hoffmann: Ông sửa được không?
Ông Bauer: Được. Ba tiếng. Vài phím không đúng. Si và Đô thăng ở giữa. Tôi sẽ làm chậm.
Bà Hoffmann: Tôi hỏi một câu được không?
Ông Bauer: Bà hỏi đi.
Bà Hoffmann: Sau khi ông chỉnh xong — tôi có phải chơi nó không?
Ông Bauer: Không.
Bà Hoffmann: Thật ạ?
Ông Bauer: Một cây đàn không cần được chơi. Nó cần đúng âm. Có người chỉnh dây xong rồi không bao giờ chạm vào nữa. Đàn sẵn sàng. Người chưa sẵn sàng. Cả hai đều ổn.
Bà Hoffmann: Tôi không chơi.
Ông Bauer: Tôi biết.
Bà Hoffmann: Tôi gọi ông vì cháu gái tôi sắp đến. Cháu mười hai tuổi. Mới bắt đầu học. Tôi muốn cháu tìm thấy một cây đàn đúng âm.
Ông Bauer: Lý do tốt.
Bà Hoffmann: Khi cháu đến, đàn sẽ sẵn sàng. Tôi không cần phải chơi.
Ông Bauer: Bà không cần. Giờ thì — hai tách cà phê. Tôi làm việc tốt hơn khi có cà phê.

options: In the living room, by the window
questions.explanation: 'In the living room. By the window.'

questions.prompt: Who used to tune and play it?
options: Her husband — but he died last year
questions.explanation: 'My husband tuned it himself. He played it. He died last year.'
questions.hint: Mrs Hoffmann's reply to 'When was it last tuned?'

questions.prompt: How long has the piano not been played?
questions.explanation: 'The piano has not been played for fourteen months.'

questions.prompt: What two keys does Mr Bauer mention?
options: B and C-sharp in the middle
questions.explanation: 'The B and the C-sharp in the middle.'
questions.hint: Mr Bauer's reply about the job.

questions.prompt: How long will the work take?
What does Mrs Hoffmann ask Mr Bauer?
questions.hint: Look at Mrs Hoffmann's 'one thing' question.

questions.prompt: How does Mr Bauer answer her question?
options: 'No. A piano does not need to be played; it needs to be in tune. Both are okay.'

questions.prompt: Why did Mrs Hoffmann call the tuner?
options: Her granddaughter (twelve, just started lessons) is coming, and she wants the granddaughter to find a piano that is in tune.
She wants to learn to play.
The piano is broken in a serious way.
questions.explanation: Mrs Hoffmann's long reply about her granddaughter.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Keeping something in working order can be an act of preparation for someone else, even if you yourself never use it.
Tuners should not work for old people.
questions.explanation: Mrs Hoffmann maintains the piano not for herself but for her granddaughter.
questions.hint: Think about who the piano is really for.

blanks.explanation: He asks where the piano is.
Do I have to play it?
It needs to be in tune.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Bauer tells Mrs Hoffmann a piano can be kept in tune for someone else, not for the person who paid for it. Is there something you have maintained — a recipe, a garden, a tool, a holiday tradition — that you are quietly keeping ready for someone who has not yet arrived?
